headline: Trout Dominates, Gausman Dazzles in a Losing Effort, and the League's Best Teams Begin to Separate
subheadline: Week 1 of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball delivered star performances, a gut-punch loss for Hiroshima, and a clear picture of which rosters are built to last.
body:
**Stat of the Week: 0.17 — Kevin Gausman posted a jaw-dropping 0.17 WHIP across 6.0 innings for the Buttville Skidmarks, one of the most dominant individual pitching performances of the young season, yet his team still lost 7–3.**

There are weeks in fantasy baseball when the numbers tell one story and the scoreboard tells another. Week 1 of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball told both simultaneously — and the dissonance was, at times, breathtaking.

## At-a-Glance

| Winner | Loser | Score | Player of the Matchup |
|--------|-------|-------|-----------------------|
| Albuquerque Bombers | Abbotsford-Colby Twins | 6-4 | Kyle Harrison (12 K, 0.33 WHIP) |
| Athletic Park Dragons | Hiroshima Radiation | 5-4 | Nolan McLean (15 K) |
| BaseballPro77 | Canberra Wombats | 8-2 | Mike Trout (3 HR, 6 R, 5 RBI, .667 OBP) |
| Benchwarmers | Buttville Skidmarks | 7-3 | Zach Neto (7 R, 2 HR) |
| General Hospital | Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs | 4-4 TIE | — (tie; no individual lifted above the result) |
| North Correa | Wisconsin Rapids Reapers | 7-3 | Chris Sale (15 K, 0.75 WHIP) |
| Sugar Land Skeeters | Riverside Roughnecks | 9-1 | José Abreu / Nolan Schanuel / Baldwin (combined 3 HR, team 8 HR, 38 K) |

---

## Thriller of the Week

**Athletic Park Dragons** def. **Hiroshima Radiation**, 5–4

By nearly every meaningful pitching measure, **Hiroshima Radiation** was the better team this week. Their 2.35 ERA dwarfed the **Dragons'** 4.10. Their 1.12 WHIP was substantially cleaner than Athletic Park's 1.34. And Nolan McLean delivered a 15-strikeout performance that would anchor most winning efforts. Yet the final score read 5–4 in favor of **Athletic Park**, who captured enough offensive categories — four home runs, 16 RBI, four stolen bases — to offset their pitching disadvantages. For **Hiroshima**, this is the kind of loss that lingers. They did nearly everything right and still walked away empty-handed, falling to 0–1 while **Athletic Park** moves to 1–0 with questions still unresolved about their pitching staff's durability.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

The most complete and commanding performance of Week 1 was not particularly close. **BaseballPro77** dismantled the **Canberra Wombats** by a score of 8–2, and the margin flattered Canberra.

At the center of it was Mike Trout, who delivered the kind of week that reminds this league — and frankly any league — why he remains one of the most dangerous fantasy assets in existence. Six runs scored. Three home runs. Five RBI. A .667 on-base percentage. These are not numbers generated across a month; they were produced in a single week, against a single opponent, in a contest that was decided early and decisively. Munetaka Murakami added three home runs of his own, giving **BaseballPro77** eight on the week — a figure that overwhelmed virtually every offensive category on offer.

The **Canberra Wombats**, for their part, received a quality start-caliber effort from Dylan Cease and showed some life on the basepaths with five stolen bases. But the absence of power — zero home runs as a team — is not merely a bad week. It is a structural indictment. In a format that rewards run production and extra-base authority, a team that cannot go deep will struggle to win the categories that matter most. Aaron Nola's 8.38 ERA compounded the damage, and **Canberra** enters Week 2 facing serious questions about both roster depth and competitive direction.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 6–4

**Albuquerque** posted 19 runs on 32 hits and received a masterful outing from Kyle Harrison — 12 strikeouts, a 0.33 WHIP — to carry a 6–4 victory. **Abbotsford-Colby** countered with genuine pop (6 HR, 5 SB, 18 RBI), but a 6.00 ERA with zero quality starts made pitching categories nearly untenable. The addition of Merrill Kelly is a sensible corrective for **Abbotsford**, but the urgency is real. **Albuquerque** heads into Week 2 with momentum and a pitching staff that appears to be hitting its stride.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Hiroshima Radiation, 5–4

Detailed above in the Thriller section. **Athletic Park** should not feel entirely comfortable at 5–4; their 4.10 ERA and 1.34 WHIP are liabilities against better-constructed pitching opponents. **Hiroshima**, meanwhile, has the arms to compete every week — the offense must find a way to generate stolen bases and runs at a higher clip to avoid more cruel defeats like this one.

### BaseballPro77 def. Canberra Wombats, 8–2

Detailed above in The Week's Defining Moment. **BaseballPro77** is 1–0 and trending toward the top of the league. **Canberra** must address its power vacuum before the season slips beyond reach.

### Benchwarmers def. Buttville Skidmarks, 7–3

This was, in many respects, a victory the **Benchwarmers** had little business claiming on pure roster quality. Zach Neto was exceptional — seven runs, two home runs — and the offense's 19 runs were enough to paper over a pitching staff that posted a 4.50 ERA, zero quality starts, and only 26 strikeouts. **Buttville**, meanwhile, watched Kevin Gausman post a historically efficient line (0.17 WHIP, 6.0 IP) and still lose the week. Chase DeLauter's four home runs gave the team legitimate power. The talent in **Buttville** is real; the surrounding infrastructure is not yet reliable. **Benchwarmers** should enjoy the win — and then quietly address their rotation before opponents adjust.

### General Hospital 4 vs. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs 4 — TIE

Both teams can make a reasonable case they deserved more. **General Hospital** posted a .414 OBP, 23 RBI, six home runs, and three quality starts — a résumé that wins most matchups in most weeks. The **Wonderdogs** answered with seven home runs, a 2.98 ERA, and three quality starts of their own. Neither team pulled away. The tie is ultimately a footnote on what both rosters are capable of, and Week 2 will tell us whether these results were representative or anomalous.

### North Correa def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7–3

Chris Sale's 15-strikeout, 0.75 WHIP performance anchored **North Correa's** week, and the offense — led by Randy Arozarena and Mookie Betts — provided enough run support to secure a convincing 7–3 result. **Wisconsin Rapids** has a real asset in Bryan Woo (0.83 WHIP, five NSVH), but Juan Soto's continued underperformance and a 5.79 ERA represent compounding problems. **North Correa** is quietly one of the most balanced teams in the league, and the power rankings reflect that reality.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Riverside Roughnecks, 9–1

The most lopsided result of the week was also the least surprising. **Sugar Land** — the league's presumptive frontrunner — deployed eight home runs, 38 strikeouts, and a 4.13 ERA to dismantle the **Roughnecks** in every meaningful category. Three separate contributors went deep. **Riverside's** 7.91 ERA and zero quality starts speak for themselves. Shea Langeliers and Royce Lewis offer hope going forward, but this rotation requires immediate and significant reconstruction.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers.** A 7–3 victory built on 19 runs and Zach Neto's production is worth celebrating — but the **Benchwarmers** won pitching categories against a **Buttville** squad that actually fielded the superior arm on the week in Kevin Gausman. A 4.50 ERA, zero quality starts, and 26 strikeouts will not hold up against the league's better-constructed offenses. The win is real. The warning signs are equally real.

**Unlucky Team: Hiroshima Radiation.** There is no graceful way to lose 5–4 when you outpitch your opponent in ERA, WHIP, and strikeouts. Nolan McLean posted 15 punchouts. The staff allowed fewer than two and a half earned runs per nine innings. And yet **Hiroshima** falls to 0–1, a victim of the format's occasional indifference to merit. They will be fine. This week simply was not fair.

---

## League Record

**General Hospital** tied the all-time highest OBP record in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball this week with a .414 mark — matching their own previous record set in Week 1. The fact that this historic offensive efficiency produced only a tie speaks more to the **Wonderdogs'** resilience than to any failing of **General Hospital's** lineup.

---

## Manager Spotlight: North Correa

**North Correa** enters the record books at 1–0 following a 7–3 win over **Wisconsin Rapids**, but what is more noteworthy than the outcome is the manner in which they achieved it. Chris Sale's 15-strikeout, 0.75 WHIP masterclass was not a fluke — it was the product of a carefully constructed rotation that, through one week, looks like the most balanced pitching staff in the league.

The offense, anchored by Mookie Betts and Randy Arozarena, is not flashy. But it scores runs in bunches — 23 on the week — and contributes across multiple categories without relying on a single transcendent performance. That kind of distributed production is sustainable in a way that feast-or-famine rosters rarely are.

**North Correa** sits fourth in the power rankings and, if Sale continues anywhere near his current trajectory, has a legitimate case to climb higher. Quietly, methodically, without generating the headlines that Trout or DeLauter command, this roster is building something worth watching.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Mike Trout (BaseballPro77):** Three home runs, six runs, five RBI, and a .667 OBP in a single week. **BUY** — if you can acquire him, the cost will be steep and worth it; this performance is not an outlier for a player of his caliber.

**Kevin Gausman (Buttville Skidmarks):** A 0.17 WHIP across six innings is historically elite, yet the surrounding roster could not capitalize. **HOLD** — Gausman's individual value is unimpeachable, but **Buttville** owners should monitor whether this talent is being maximized or wasted.

**Juan Soto (Wisconsin Rapids Reapers):** The numbers through Week 1 are quietly alarming for a player of Soto's standing. **BUY (if you're not his owner)** — the underlying talent demands a correction, and the manager holding him may be growing impatient enough to sell at a discount.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Dominant 9–1 blowout with 8 HR, 38 K, and three contributors (Abreu, Schanuel, Baldwin) each going deep — the most complete team in the league through one week | ➡ same
2. **BaseballPro77** | Trout's 3-HR, .667 OBP explosion and Murakami's 3 HR made this an 8–2 rout; consistent elite production in all three phases | ⬆ up
3. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Survived a near-upset with a 5–4 squeaker; Gleyber Torres and Andrés Giménez were excellent, but that ERA needs cleaning up | ⬇ down
4. **North Correa** | Chris Sale's 15 K, 0.75 WHIP masterclass anchored a 7–3 win; Arozarena, Betts providing steady offense — quietly the most balanced staff in the league | ⬆ up
5. **Benchwarmers** | Zach Neto's 7-run, 2-HR week is a revelation and the 7–3 win is real, but 0 quality starts and a 4.50 ERA will catch up to them against tougher opponents | ⬆ up
6. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Kyle Harrison's 12-K, 0.33 WHIP gem carried a 6–4 win; Yelich and Turang contributing — this is a team trending the right direction after waiver activity | ⬆ up
7. **General Hospital** | Tied 4–4 despite posting a .414 OBP, 23 RBI, and 3 QS — this roster is legitimately good and the tie is a fluke against a scrappy Wonderdogs team | ⬇ down
8. **Hiroshima Radiation** | The 5–4 loss to the Dragons is a gut-punch considering they dominated pitching categories; McLean's 15 Ks show real ceiling | ➡ same
9. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Tied 4–4 in a game they probably deserved to lose — 3 QS and a 2.98 ERA are genuine strengths but the offense remains structurally thin | ⬆ up
10. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Lost 6–4 but posted 6 HR, 5 SB, and Yordan Alvarez and Jonathan Aranda are real weapons; adding Merrill Kelly is a smart pitching fix | ➡ same
11. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Lost 7–3 but Bryan Woo's 0.83 WHIP and 5 NSVH show this team can win pitching weeks — Juan Soto is criminally underperforming so far | ⬇ down
12. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Chase DeLauter's 4-HR week and Gausman's 0.17 WHIP are stars on a 3–7 team — the talent is there but the surrounding roster is too inconsistent | ➡ same
13. **Canberra Wombats** | Dylan Cease was excellent and still lost 8–2; zero home runs as a team is a death sentence in this format and the lineup has no answer | ➡ same
14. **Riverside Roughnecks** | A 1–9 loss with a 7.91 ERA and 0 quality starts is as bad as it gets; Shea Langeliers and Royce Lewis provide some hope but the pitching is a disaster | ⬇ down

---

## Waiver Wire

No notable moves were recorded this week. Managers are advised to monitor the injury wire closely heading into Week 2, as roster construction decisions made in these early weeks carry disproportionate weight as the season develops.
